attributes.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with a generalised tonic-clonic seizure at 34 weeks gestation. She has a medical history of gestational hypertension diagnosed two weeks ago. She takes no regular medications. On examination, she appears postictal. Her pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 180/110 mmHg, respiratory rate 22/min, SpO2 95% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. Urinalysis reveals 3+ proteinuria. Foetal heart rate is 160 beats per minute.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with eclampsia, characterised by a seizure in the context of pre-eclampsia (hypertension and proteinuria in pregnancy). The most appropriate next step in management is to administer magnesium sulfate. Magnesium sulfate is the first-line treatment for eclampsia as it prevents and controls seizures more effectively than other anticonvulsants. It also provides neuroprotection for the fetus.

Performing an emergency caesarean section is ultimately necessary for definitive treatment of eclampsia, but it is not the immediate next step. The patient's condition needs to be stabilised first with anticonvulsant therapy and blood pressure control before proceeding to delivery.

Starting a labetalol infusion can be an important part of managing eclampsia to control blood pressure, but it is secondary to seizure prevention and control. Once magnesium sulfate has been administered, antihypertensive therapy should be initiated to maintain systolic blood pressure between 140-160 mmHg and diastolic blood pressure between 90-105 mmHg.

Administering diazepam is not the preferred first-line treatment for eclamptic seizures. While benzodiazepines can be used to control seizures, magnesium sulfate has been shown to be more effective in preventing recurrent seizures in eclampsia and has a better safety profile for both mother and fetus.

Administering midazolam, another benzodiazepine, is also not the first-line treatment for eclamptic seizures. Like diazepam, it is less effective than magnesium sulfate in preventing recurrent seizures in eclampsia.

After administering magnesium sulfate and controlling blood pressure, the next steps would include close maternal and fetal monitoring, fluid management, and planning for delivery. The mode and timing of delivery would depend on the gestational age, severity of the condition, and response to initial management. In eclampsia, delivery is usually expedited regardless of gestational age, as it is the only definitive treatment.
learningPoints: Magnesium sulfate is the first-line treatment for eclampsia to prevent and control seizures.
Delivery of the baby and placenta is the definitive treatment for eclampsia, regardless of gestational age.
Blood pressure control with antihypertensive medications is crucial in managing eclampsia to prevent complications.
references: Society of Obstetric Medicine of Australia and New Zealand (SOMANZ). Hypertension in Pregnancy Guideline 2023 [Internet]. Updated 30 June 2023. Available from: https://www.somanz.org/content/uploads/2023/06/SOMANZ_Hypertension_in_Pregnancy_Guideline_2023_Updated_30.6.23.pdf
